message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it from the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Find the code path/contract/test that makes the PoC impossible or moot. Only survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

O-3: Integration gate reports green even when the postgres/qdrant service containers never came up — live tests silently skip
File: .github/workflows/ci.yml:44
Description: The integration job declares postgres:16 and qdrant service containers and the workflow comment asserts the live checkpointer + vector-store tests 'run for real via the service containers'. But every live test is guarded by skipif(not postgres_up()/qdrant_up()), which probe localhost:5432 / :6333 with a 1s TCP connect and SKIP (not fail) when unreachable. If a service image fails to pull, the health check never passes, or the port mapping breaks, all 12+ live persistence/observability tests skip and pytest still exits 0 — so the integration job (a required check via all-checks) goes green while exercising none of the real backends it claims to cover. The reproducibility/coverage guarantee is unenforced.
PoC: In tests/integration/conftest.py postgres_up()/qdrant_up() return False on any OSError; the live tests use @pytest.mark.skipif(not postgres_up(), ...). grep shows no ASSAY_REQUIRE_*/xfail_strict/no-skip enforcement in CI or pyproject. Break the qdrant service port mapping in ci.yml -> test_qdrant_* SKIP, job still succeeds.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate this finding about the CI integration gate reporting green when service containers fail to start.

content.content:
1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
22	        run: |
23	          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
24	          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
25	          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
26	          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
27	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
28	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
29	          pip install --no-deps -e .
30	      - name: Lint (ruff)
31	        run: ruff check src tests
32	      - name: Format check (ruff)
33	        run: ruff format --check src tests
34	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
35	        run: mypy
36	      - name: Test (pytest)
37	        run: pytest -q
38	
39	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
40	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
41	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
42	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
43	  # e2e) run for real.
44	  integration:
45	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
46	    services:
47	      postgres:
48	        image: postgres:16
49	        env:
50	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
51	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
52	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
53	        options: >-
54	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
55	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
56	      qdrant:
57	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
58	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
59	    env:
60	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
61	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/assay
62	    steps:
63	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
64	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
65	        with:
66	          python-version: "3.12"
67	      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
68	        run: |
69	          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
70	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
71	          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
72	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
73	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
74	          pip install --no-deps -e .
75	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
76	        run: mypy
77	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
78	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
79	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
80	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
81	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
82	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
83	
84	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
85	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
86	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
87	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
88	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
89	  audit:
90	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
91	    steps:
92	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
93	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
94	        with:
95	          python-version: "3.12"
96	      - name: Install pip-audit
97	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
98	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
99	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
100	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
101	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
102	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
103	        run: |
104	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
105	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
106	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
107	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
108	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
109	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
110	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
111	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
112	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
113	          # stay in sync too.
114	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
115	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
116	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
117	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
118	        run: |
119	          set -euo pipefail
120	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
121	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
122	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
123	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
124	          IGNORES=()
125	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
126	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
127	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
128	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
129	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
130	          fi
131	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
132	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
133	        run: |
134	          set -euo pipefail
135	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
136	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
137	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
138	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
139	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
140	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
141	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
142	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
143	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
144	          IGNORES=()
145	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
146	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
147	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
148	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
149	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
150	          fi
151	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
152	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
153	          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
154	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
155	        if: always()
156	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
157	        with:
158	          name: sbom-cyclonedx
159	          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
160	          if-no-files-found: error
161	      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
162	        run: |
163	          set -euo pipefail
164	          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
165	          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
166	          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
167	          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
168	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
169	          pip install --no-deps -e .
170	          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
171	          pip-licenses --format=json --with-system > licenses.json
172	          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
173	      - name: Upload license report
174	        if: always()
175	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
176	        with:
177	          name: license-report
178	          path: licenses.json
179	          if-no-files-found: warn
180	
181	  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
182	  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
183	  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
184	  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
185	  all-checks:
186	    if: always()
187	    needs: [core, integration, audit]
188	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
189	    steps:
190	      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
191	        run: |
192	          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
193	          echo "job results: $results"
194	          for r in $results; do
195	            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
196	              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
197	              exit 1
198	            fi
199	          done
200	          echo "all required jobs succeeded"
201

1	"""Integration-test helpers — exercise the REAL backends, skip cleanly when absent.
2	
3	These tests run the actual seams against real langgraph / mlflow / opentelemetry / ollama /
4	qdrant / postgres rather than mocks, so the backend code paths (the per-seam audits' coverage
5	gaps) are genuinely executed. Each skips when its extra or live service is unavailable, so the
6	dependency-free `.[dev]` lane still passes; the extras CI lane runs them for real.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import importlib.util
12	import socket
13	import urllib.request
14	
15	import pytest
16	
17	
18	def have(module: str) -> bool:
19	    return importlib.util.find_spec(module) is not None
20	
21	
22	def tcp_open(host: str, port: int, timeout: float = 1.0) -> bool:
23	    try:
24	        with socket.create_connection((host, port), timeout=timeout):
25	            return True
26	    except OSError:
27	        return False
28	
29	
30	def http_ok(url: str, timeout: float = 2.0) -> bool:
31	    try:
32	        with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=timeout) as resp:
33	            return 200 <= resp.status < 500
34	    except Exception:
35	        return False
36	
37	
38	def ollama_up() -> bool:
39	    return have("langchain_ollama") and http_ok("http://localhost:11434/api/tags")
40	
41	
42	def qdrant_up() -> bool:
43	    return have("qdrant_client") and tcp_open("localhost", 6333)
44	
45	
46	def postgres_up() -> bool:
47	    return have("psycopg") and tcp_open("localhost", 5432)
48	
49	
50\[REDACTED]
51	def require_ollama():
52	    if not ollama_up():
53	        pytest.skip("ollama not available on localhost:11434")
54

content.text: Both claims confirmed: tests use `@pytest.mark.skipif(not postgres_up()/qdrant_up())` and there is zero enforcement (`ASSAY_REQUIRE_*`, xfail_strict, no-skip) anywhere. Let me check pyproject's pytest config and whether there's any "must run" assertion test, and confirm the all-checks gate keys only off job `result` (success), not test counts.

content.text:
Confirmed. The pytest config has no `addopts` with strict skip handling, no `-W error`, no enforcement. The `filterwarnings` entries are unrelated. No sentinel/canary test asserts that the services actually came up.

Let me verify the final piece: that breaking the qdrant port mapping would indeed only cause skips (not a collection error), and that the `all-checks` gate only checks job `result == success`. I've already seen the gate logic at lines 192-199 — it keys purely off `needs.*.result`, never test counts. Let me confirm the live test imports degrade to skip rather than erroring at collection.

content.content:
1	"""Live persistence — real LangGraph MemorySaver round-trip; Qdrant/Postgres if running."""
2	
3	from __future__ import annotations
4	
5	import pytest
6	
7	from tests.integration.conftest import have, postgres_up, qdrant_up
8	
9	
10\[REDACTED](not have("langgraph"), reason="persistence extra not installed")
11	def test_memory_checkpointer_persists_graph_state():
12	    # Test the checkpointer the way it is actually used: through a compiled graph, asserting
13	    # state is durably persisted under the thread_id and readable via get_state.
14	    from typing import TypedDict
15	
16	    from langgraph.graph import END, START, StateGraph
17	
18	    from assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint import configured_checkpointer
19	
20	    class S(TypedDict):
21	        n: int
22	
23	    cp = configured_checkpointer(use_memory=True)
24	    b = StateGraph(S)
25	    b.add_node("inc", lambda s: {"n": s["n"] + 1})
26	    b.add_edge(START, "inc")
27	    b.add_edge("inc", END)
28	    graph = b.compile(checkpointer=cp)
29	
30	    cfg = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "t1"}}
31	    graph.invoke({"n": 0}, cfg)

content.text:
I have everything needed. The finding is fully confirmed and I cannot disprove it.

The mechanism is airtight against my refutation attempts:

1. **Skip, not fail**: `test_persistence_live.py` line 36/48/76/111/148/184 all use `@pytest.mark.skipif(not qdrant_up()/postgres_up(), ...)`. `skipif` produces SKIP outcomes, which pytest counts as success (exit 0). Line 44-45 even adds a defensive inner `pytest.skip` if the qdrant container is cold — further biasing toward skip over fail.

2. **Probes are best-effort and swallow all errors**: `tcp_open` (conftest.py:22-27) returns `False` on any `OSError`. A broken port mapping, failed image pull, or unhealthy container all yield `False` → skip.

3. **No enforcement anywhere**: grep found zero `ASSAY_REQUIRE_*`, no `xfail_strict`, no `--runxfail`, no `-W error` for skips, no sentinel/canary test asserting services came up. The `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` block has no `addopts` enforcing non-skip. The `filterwarnings` entries are unrelated.

4. **The gate keys only off job result**: `all-checks` (ci.yml:185-200) checks `needs.integration.result == "success"`. The `integration` job's success is determined solely by `pytest -q ... ` exit code (line 82), which is 0 when tests skip. Nothing inspects skip/collected counts.

5. **The qdrant service has no health check** (ci.yml:56-58, unlike postgres which has `--health-cmd`), so a port-mapping break wouldn't even be caught at the service-startup level — the job proceeds and the tests silently skip.

The PoC ("Break the qdrant service port mapping in ci.yml -> test_qdrant_* SKIP, job still succeeds") is directly reproducible from the code. The workflow comment (lines 77-79) asserting these "run for real via the service containers" is an unenforced claim.

input.reasoning: Confirmed and cannot disprove. tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py guards all 6 live postgres/qdrant tests (lines 36,48,76,111,148,184) with @pytest.mark.skipif(not qdrant_up()/postgres_up(), ...) — skipif yields SKIP, which pytest counts toward exit 0, not failure. conftest.py tcp_open() (lines 22-27) returns False on ANY OSError, so a failed image pull, broken port mapping, or unhealthy container all collapse to a skip. The qdrant service (ci.yml:56-58) has NO health check (unlike postgres at 53-55), so a port-mapping break is never caught at startup either. grep found ZERO enforcement: no ASSAY_REQUIRE_*, no xfail_strict, no -W error, no --runxfail, no sentinel/canary asserting the services came up; pyproject [tool.pytest.ini_options] has no addopts enforcing non-skip (only unrelated filterwarnings). The required check all-checks (ci.yml:185-200) evaluates ONLY needs.integration.result=='success', and the integration job's success is the pytest exit code from line 82, which is 0 when tests skip — nothing inspects skip/collected counts. The PoC (break qdrant port -> tests SKIP -> job green) is directly reproducible. The workflow comment (lines 77-79) claiming these run 'for real via the service containers' is an unenforced assertion.

content.text:
I confirmed finding O-3 and could not disprove it. The integration gate genuinely reports green even when the postgres/qdrant service containers never come up, because all live tests skip silently and nothing in CI or pytest config enforces that they actually run.

Key evidence (all absolute paths):
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml:36-58 (skip-on-absent comment + qdrant service with no health check), :82 (pytest exit code is the only success signal), :185-200 (`all-checks` keys only off `needs.integration.result == "success"`)
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/integration/conftest.py:22-47 (`tcp_open` returns False on any OSError; `postgres_up`/`qdrant_up` built on it)
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py:36,48,76,111,148,184 (all live tests use `@pytest.mark.skipif`, i.e. SKIP not FAIL)
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` — no `addopts`, no `xfail_strict`, no skip enforcement

grep across the repo found no `ASSAY_REQUIRE_*`, `xfail_strict`, `--runxfail`, `-W error`, or sentinel/canary test that would force the real backends to execute.
